speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! You've got a great eye – this 2014 Wrangler Sport is one of the cleanest examples I've seen come through here in months. Trail-rated 4x4 with that bulletproof 3.6L V6, removable top and doors – perfect timing with summer right around the corner. Just had a full service, runs like a dream. These things hold their value better than almost anything on the road. What brings you in today – daily driver or weekend adventure rig?

text: Hey there! You've got a great eye – this 2014 Wrangler Sport is one of the cleanest examples I've seen come through here in months. Trail-rated 4x4 with that bulletproof 3.6L V6, removable top and doors – perfect timing with summer right around the corner. Just had a full service, runs like a dream. These things hold their value better than almost anything on the road. What brings you in today – daily driver or weekend adventure rig?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Thanks for the pitch! I'm looking for something reliable I can drive year-round, but I'd definitely enjoy the removable top in good weather. Before I dig deeper – can you walk me through the maintenance history? You mentioned a full service recently – what was done, and what's the service record look like overall? Also, has this Wrangler been in any accidents?

text: Thanks for the pitch! I'm looking for something reliable I can drive year-round, but I'd definitely enjoy the removable top in good weather. Before I dig deeper – can you walk me through the maintenance history? You mentioned a full service recently – what was done, and what's the service record look like overall? Also, has this Wrangler been in any accidents?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Great questions – I love a buyer who does their homework! So this Wrangler has been meticulously maintained. The recent service included a full synthetic oil change, new brake pads up front, tire rotation, and a complete fluid check – transmission, differential, coolant, the works. Previous owner kept up with it religiously, we've got records showing regular oil changes every 5,000 miles or so.

As for accidents – this one's clean. I pulled the history myself when we took it in on trade. No accidents reported, no structural damage, nothing like that. These Wranglers are built like tanks, and this one's been treated right. You can see the frame underneath is solid, no rust issues which is huge for these older Jeeps.

The 3.6L Pentastar in these is known to go 200,000+ miles easy if you take care of it, and this one's only at 118k – really just getting broken in for a Jeep. You planning to do any off-roading, or mostly stick to pavement?

As for accidents – this one's clean. I pulled the history myself when we took it in on trade. No accidents reported, no structural damage, nothing like that. These Wranglers are built like tanks, and this one's been treated right. You can see the frame underneath is solid, no rust issues which is huge for these older Jeeps.

The 3.6L Pentastar in these is known to go 200,000+ miles easy if you take care of it, and this one's only at 118k – really just getting broken in for a Jeep. You planning to do any off-roading, or mostly stick to pavement?

speaker: buyer

args.message: That's reassuring about the maintenance and accident history – I appreciate you pulling the records. A couple more things: At 118k miles, has anything major ever needed replacement? Transmission, water pump, any of the typical wear items? And I'm curious – what was the trade-in situation? How long did the previous owner have it, and do you know why they traded it in?

text: That's reassuring about the maintenance and accident history – I appreciate you pulling the records. A couple more things: At 118k miles, has anything major ever needed replacement? Transmission, water pump, any of the typical wear items? And I'm curious – what was the trade-in situation? How long did the previous owner have it, and do you know why they traded it in?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Yeah, absolutely – happy to share what I know. At 118k, this one's been pretty straightforward. No major replacements needed – transmission shifts smooth as butter, water pump is original and no signs of leaks or issues there. The previous owner did the usual wear items – brakes, spark plugs, that kind of thing – but nothing out of the ordinary for a Jeep with this mileage. That's actually a testament to how well it was maintained.

As for the trade-in story – the previous owner was a gentleman in his early 50s, had the Jeep for about four years. Said he loved it, but his kids moved out and he wanted something more fuel-efficient for his commute. You know how it goes – life changes, needs change. He was actually a little sad to see it go, honestly. Asked me to find it a good home, so here we are!

He used it mostly for weekend trips, some light trail stuff up north, but nothing crazy. That's actually the sweet spot for these – enough use to keep everything lubricated and running right, but not beaten on. 

You want to take it for a spin? That's really where you'll feel how solid this thing is. The drivetrain is tight, no weird noises, shifts perfectly. I can grab the keys right now if you're ready.

speaker: buyer

args.message: I'd definitely like to take it for a test drive – that's always the best way to get a feel for a car. But before we do that, let me ask about pricing. The asking price is $19,995. Given the mileage, condition, and maintenance history you've described, is there any room to negotiate on that number, or is that pretty firm?

text: I'd definitely like to take it for a test drive – that's always the best way to get a feel for a car. But before we do that, let me ask about pricing. The asking price is $19,995. Given the mileage, condition, and maintenance history you've described, is there any room to negotiate on that number, or is that pretty firm?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Ha, I appreciate you being direct – I respect that! Look, I'll be honest with you: Wranglers are a different animal when it comes to pricing. They hold value like nothing else on the road. I was just at auction last week and saw a 2014 Sport with 130k miles – more miles than this one – go for over $20,500. These things are in demand, especially heading into summer.

That said, I'm not here to play games with you. The $19,995 is competitive for what you're getting – clean history, well-maintained, ready to roll. But I always say let's take the drive first, let the Jeep sell itself, and then we can sit down and talk numbers. If you're serious and we can do a deal today, I've got a little flexibility – nothing crazy, but I want to earn your business.

Fair enough? Let me grab those keys and let's see how she feels on the road. I think once you're behind the wheel, you'll see why these things command the prices they do.

speaker: buyer

args.message: That makes sense – I'm happy to take it out and see how it feels. Just one quick thing before we go: you mentioned you have "a little flexibility" on price if we do a deal today. Ballpark – are we talking a couple hundred dollars off, or is there more room there? I'm trying to get a sense of what "negotiable" actually means so I'm not wasting both our time.

text: That makes sense – I'm happy to take it out and see how it feels. Just one quick thing before we go: you mentioned you have "a little flexibility" on price if we do a deal today. Ballpark – are we talking a couple hundred dollars off, or is there more room there? I'm trying to get a sense of what "negotiable" actually means so I'm not wasting both our time.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Fair enough, I can respect that – you want to know if we're in the same ballpark before investing more time. I get it.

Look, realistically? If you're a serious buyer and we can shake hands today, I could probably move a few hundred – maybe $300, $400 off to make it happen. We're not talking thousands here because honestly, at $19,995 we're already priced to sell. I've seen dealers asking $21k, $22k for comparable Wranglers with similar miles.

But here's the thing – take the drive, fall in love with it, and then let's talk. If you come back and make me a reasonable offer, I'll take it to my manager and fight for you. I want to move this Jeep to the right person, and you seem like someone who'll actually appreciate it.

So what do you say – ready to see what 285 horsepower of trail-rated fun feels like? Keys are right inside, I'll grab 'em.

So what do you say – ready to see what 285 horsepower of trail-rated fun feels like? Keys are right inside, I'll grab 'em.

speaker: buyer

args.message: Alright, let's do it – I'm ready for the test drive. Before we head out though, one last thing: how does this Wrangler do on fuel economy? I know these aren't known for sipping gas, but what kind of real-world MPG should I expect with highway and city driving?

text: Alright, let's do it – I'm ready for the test drive. Before we head out though, one last thing: how does this Wrangler do on fuel economy? I know these aren't known for sipping gas, but what kind of real-world MPG should I expect with highway and city driving?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Ha, yeah, you're not buying a Prius here, that's for sure! But honestly, for what you're getting – a true 4x4 with this kind of capability – it's really not bad at all.

Real-world, you're looking at about 16-17 in the city, and on the highway you can get 20-21 if you keep your foot out of it. The 3.6L V6 is actually pretty efficient for the power it puts out. Previous owner mentioned he was averaging around 18-19 combined, which tracks with what I've seen from these.

The trick with Wranglers is they're boxy, so wind resistance hits you a bit on the highway – but that's also what makes them look so iconic, right? Trade-offs. And honestly, with gas prices where they are now, the difference between this and a sedan is maybe $20-30 more a month. Small price to pay for the freedom of ripping the top off on a nice day.

Alright, let's do this – follow me inside and I'll grab the keys. You're gonna love how this thing drives.

speaker: buyer

args.message: Sounds good. One more quick one – what's the title situation? Is it clean and clear, no liens or anything like that? Just want to make sure everything's straightforward before I commit any time.

text: Sounds good. One more quick one – what's the title situation? Is it clean and clear, no liens or anything like that? Just want to make sure everything's straightforward before I commit any time.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Oh, absolutely – clean title, no liens, completely clear. We don't put anything on the lot without making sure the paperwork is squared away first. Title's in hand, ready to transfer the minute you decide to pull the trigger.

No salvage, no rebuilt, no funny business – just a straightforward clean title. That's actually one of the first things I check when we take a vehicle in on trade. You'd be surprised how many dealers skip that step and then have headaches later. Not us.

So you're good there – smooth transaction, no surprises. Alright, let's get you behind the wheel! Right this way.

speaker: buyer

args.message: Perfect – I appreciate that. Alright, let's take it out and see how it drives. I'll do a quick spin and get a feel for the condition and how everything handles.

text: Perfect – I appreciate that. Alright, let's take it out and see how it drives. I'll do a quick spin and get a feel for the condition and how everything handles.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Awesome, let's do it! Here are the keys – fire it up and let it idle for a sec so you can hear how smooth that V6 runs. No ticks, no knocks, just that nice Pentastar purr.

I'd suggest taking a right out of the lot, there's a nice stretch of road about a mile down where you can open it up a little and feel the power. Then loop back through some side streets so you can test the steering and brakes at lower speeds. Maybe hit a couple bumps – you'll feel how tight the suspension is.

Pay attention to the transmission – smooth shifts, no hesitation. And when you're ready, throw it in 4-wheel drive just to see how easy it engages. These things are built for capability, but they're surprisingly comfortable as daily drivers too.

Alright, hop in – I'll ride shotgun and answer any questions while you drive. Let's see what you think!

args.message:
Sounds good. *[After test drive]* Okay, I've had a chance to take it out and get a feel for it. The engine runs smooth, transmission shifts well, steering feels solid. Interior's clean – I can see this has been well cared for. No weird noises, no red flags I can pick up on from driving it.

Here's where I am: I like the vehicle. The maintenance history checks out, it drives well, and the price is in the ballpark. But I want to make sure we're being fair on both sides. What's the absolute best you can do on price if I'm ready to move forward today?

speaker: seller

args.message:
See, I told you it drives like a dream! Glad you felt it for yourself – that's a Jeep that's been loved.

Alright, you've been straight with me, so I'm gonna be straight with you. You're a serious buyer, you're ready to move today, and I want to make this happen. Let me go sharpen the pencil here...

Look, the best I can do is $19,500. That's $500 off the asking price, and honestly, that's leaving money on the table for me. But I'd rather see this Jeep go to someone who appreciates it than nickel-and-dime over a few hundred bucks.

At $19,500, you're getting a clean, well-maintained Wrangler that's ready for years of adventures. These things don't depreciate like normal cars – you could drive it for two years and probably sell it for close to what you paid. It's almost like putting money in the bank.

So what do you say – do we have a deal at $19,500?

That's still only asking for another $300 off your counter, and it gets us to a number that feels right to me. I'm ready to move today if we can make that work.

Let's go inside, I'll get the paperwork started, and we'll have you on the road in that Wrangler before the end of the day. Summer's right around the corner – you're gonna have a blast with this thing.
